title: SAST with SonarQube and Trivy — SAMA PARTNERS
description: An informative course on Static Application Security Testing (SAST) using SonarQube and Trivy. Learners will understand what SAST is, how each tool works, how to configure them, and how to run guided scans.
outcomes: Understand what SAST is and why it matters in a CI/CD pipeline
Explain the difference between SonarQube and Trivy and when to use each
Configure SonarQube and run a source code analysis
Run a Trivy filesystem scan to detect vulnerable dependencies and secrets
Run a Trivy container image scan and interpret the CVE results
Generate and read a security report from both tools

levels.title: Course Overview
levels.content:
# SAST with SonarQube and Trivy

Welcome to this course on **Static Application Security Testing (SAST)** using two industry-standard open-source tools: **SonarQube** and **Trivy**.

SAST allows you to find security vulnerabilities and code quality issues in your source code **before** the application is ever deployed — the earlier you catch a bug, the cheaper it is to fix.

## Course Structure

| # | Level | Type |
|:---:|---|---|
| 1 | What is SAST? | Info |
| 2 | SonarQube — What It Is and What It Does | Info |
| 3 | Trivy — What It Is and What It Does | Info |
| 4 | SonarQube Setup and First Scan | Hands-on |
| 5 | Trivy Filesystem Scan | Hands-on |
| 6 | Trivy Container Image Scan | Hands-on |
| 7 | Reading Results and Generating Reports | Info |

## Tools Covered

- **SonarQube** — static code analysis platform that inspects your source code for bugs, vulnerabilities, and code smells
- **Trivy** — all-in-one scanner that detects known CVEs in your dependencies, container images, and secrets

> **Note:** These two tools complement each other. SonarQube finds what **you wrote wrong**. Trivy finds what **your dependencies have wrong**. A complete SAST strategy uses both.

levels.title: What is SAST?
levels.content:
# What is SAST?

**Static Application Security Testing (SAST)** is the process of analyzing source code, bytecode, or binaries **without executing the application** to find security flaws, coding errors, and quality problems.

SAST tools read your code the way a security-aware senior engineer would — looking for dangerous patterns, bad configurations, and known vulnerable library versions.

## SAST vs DAST

| | SAST | DAST |
|---|---|---|
| **What it analyzes** | Source code (static) | Running application (dynamic) |
| **When it runs** | Before build / at commit | After deployment to staging |
| **Finds** | Code-level flaws, bad patterns, CVEs in deps | Runtime behavior, misconfigured headers, auth flaws |
| **Example tools** | SonarQube, Trivy | OWASP ZAP, Burp Suite |

## Why Integrate SAST in CI/CD — Shift Left

The cost of fixing a vulnerability grows the later it is discovered:

- **At development** → minutes to fix, developer has full context
- **In QA** → hours to fix, requires regression testing
- **In production** → days to fix, potential data breach, reputational damage

By embedding SAST in your CI/CD pipeline, every code push is automatically scanned — the same way unit tests run on every commit.

## What SAST Can Find

| Vulnerability Type | Example |
|---|---|
| Injection flaws | SQL injection, command injection, XSS |
| Hardcoded secrets | API keys, passwords, tokens in source code |
| Insecure dependencies | Libraries with known CVEs (e.g. log4j) |
| Code smells | Overly complex methods, duplicated blocks |
| IaC misconfigurations | Insecure Terraform or Kubernetes manifests |
| Weak cryptography | MD5 for passwords, hardcoded IV values |

levels.title: SonarQube — What It Is and What It Does
levels.content:
# SonarQube — Static Code Analysis Platform

## What Is SonarQube?

SonarQube is an **open-source static analysis platform** that inspects your source code for bugs, vulnerabilities, and maintainability issues. It supports **30+ programming languages** (Python, Java, JavaScript, Go, C/C++, PHP, and more) and integrates natively with GitLab, GitHub, Jenkins, and Azure DevOps.

## How SonarQube Works

```
Your Source Code
      |
      v
SonarScanner (runs in your CI job)
      |  reads sonar-project.properties
      |  sends analysis data over HTTPS
      v
SonarQube Server
      |  applies rules from Quality Profile
      |  evaluates Quality Gate thresholds
      v
Dashboard + Pipeline Result (PASS / FAIL)
```

## Key Concepts

| Concept | What It Means |
|---|---|
| **Quality Profile** | The collection of rules used when scanning (one per language). Controls *what* is detected. |
| **Quality Gate** | A set of pass/fail thresholds. Controls *whether the pipeline is blocked*. |
| **Code Smell** | Working code written in a way that causes future maintenance problems. Not a bug, but a risk. |
| **Vulnerability** | A code weakness that can be directly exploited by an attacker. |
| **Security Hotspot** | Code that *may* be sensitive — flagged for human review to confirm if it is genuinely vulnerable. |
| **Technical Debt** | A time estimate of how long it would take to fix all maintainability issues in the codebase. |

## Issue Severity Scale

`Info` → `Minor` → `Major` → `Critical` → `Blocker`

## Default Quality Gate — "Sonar Way"

| Condition | Threshold |
|---|---|
| New code coverage | >= 80% |
| New reliability rating | A (zero new bugs) |
| New security rating | A (zero new vulnerabilities) |
| New duplicated lines | < 3% |
| New security hotspots reviewed | 100% |

> Quality Gates apply to **new code only** — the code added since the last version. This lets teams improve incrementally without being blocked by legacy issues.

## Real-World Example — SQL Injection

SonarQube rule **`python:S3649`** flags this pattern:

```python
# VULNERABLE — user input injected directly into SQL
query = f"SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = '{user_id}'"
cursor.execute(query)
```

Fixed with a parameterized query:

```python
# SAFE — parameter sent separately, never interpolated
query = "SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = ?"
cursor.execute(query, (user_id,))
```

levels.title: Trivy — What It Is and What It Does
levels.content:
# Trivy — All-in-One Vulnerability Scanner

## What Is Trivy?

Trivy (by Aqua Security) is an **open-source, all-in-one security scanner** that detects known CVEs in OS packages, language libraries, container images, IaC files, and hardcoded secrets. It requires no configuration to get started and is widely used in CI/CD pipelines.

## What Trivy Can Scan

| Target | What Trivy Finds |
|---|---|
| **Filesystem / source code** | Vulnerable libraries (pip, npm, maven, go.mod), exposed secrets |
| **Container images** | CVEs in OS packages (apt, apk, yum) and language dependencies |
| **Git repositories** | Vulnerabilities in remote repos (no clone required) |
| **IaC files** | Misconfigurations in Terraform, Helm, Kubernetes manifests |
| **SBOM** | Generates a Software Bill of Materials (CycloneDX, SPDX) |

## How Trivy Works

```
Scan Target (e.g. ./myproject or nginx:latest)
      |
      v
Trivy downloads / updates its Vulnerability Database
  (sources: NVD, OSV, GitHub Advisory, Alpine SecDB, etc.)
      |
      v
Identifies all packages and their versions
      |
      v
Matches each package against the CVE database
      |
      v
Output: Table / JSON / SARIF / CycloneDX
```

## SonarQube vs Trivy — Side by Side

| Dimension | SonarQube | Trivy |
|---|---|---|
| **Primary Input** | Your source code (logic analysis) | Package manifests, image layers |
| **What It Finds** | Logic flaws, bad patterns, code smells | Known CVEs in third-party libraries |
| **Finds Your Bugs** | Yes | No |
| **Finds Dep CVEs** | Limited | Yes — comprehensive |
| **Container Scanning** | No | Yes |
| **Secret Detection** | Yes (with rules) | Yes (filesystem scan) |

> **Use both together.** A project can have zero SonarQube issues and still ship a container with 50 critical CVEs in its base image — and vice versa.

## Real-World Example — Vulnerable Dependency

Trivy detects:
```
Library       requests-2.28.0
CVE           CVE-2023-32681
Severity      HIGH
Fixed In      requests-2.31.0
Description   Unintended leak of Proxy-Authorization header
```

Fix in `requirements.txt`:
```
# Before
requests==2.28.0

# After
requests>=2.31.0
```

levels.title: SonarQube — Setup and First Scan
levels.content:
# SonarQube — Setup and First Scan

In this level you will start a SonarQube instance, configure your project, and run your first static code analysis.

## Scenario

Your team has a Python project and wants to start enforcing code quality and security standards using SonarQube. Your task is to bring up a SonarQube server, configure the project scanner, and run the first analysis.

---

## Step 1 — Start SonarQube with Docker

```bash
docker run -d \
  --name sonarqube \
  -p 9000:9000 \
  sonarqube:community
```

Wait ~60 seconds, then verify the server is ready:

```bash
curl -s http://localhost:9000/api/system/status | python3 -m json.tool
```

You should see `"status": "UP"` in the response.

> **Default credentials:** `admin` / `admin` — change the password on first login.

## Step 2 — Generate a Project Token

In the SonarQube UI:
```
My Account → Security → Generate Tokens
  Name: my-project-scanner
  Type: Project Analysis Token
  Copy the token — it is shown only once
```

## Step 3 — Create sonar-project.properties

Place this file at the root of your project:

```properties
sonar.projectKey=my-python-project
sonar.projectName=My Python Project
sonar.sources=src
sonar.sourceEncoding=UTF-8
sonar.exclusions=**/node_modules/**,**/vendor/**
```

## Step 4 — Run the Scanner

```bash
docker run --rm \
  -e SONAR_HOST_URL="http://host.docker.internal:9000" \
  -e SONAR_TOKEN="YOUR_TOKEN_HERE" \
  -v "$(pwd):/usr/src" \
  sonarsource/sonar-scanner-cli
```

## Step 5 — Check the Quality Gate Result via API

```bash
curl -u YOUR_TOKEN_HERE: \
  "http://localhost:9000/api/qualitygates/project_status?projectKey=my-python-project"
```

The **`status`** field will be **`OK`** (gate passed) or **`ERROR`** (gate failed).

---

> **Answer:** SonarQube listens on a specific default port. Submit that port number as the answer.
levels.solution:
SonarQube's default web port is **`9000`**. This is the port exposed in the `docker run` command (`-p 9000:9000`) and the port used in all API calls (`http://localhost:9000/...`).

Submit **`9000`** as the answer.

hints.title: Where to find the port
hints.content: Look at the `docker run` command in Step 1. The `-p` flag maps a host port to a container port in the format `host_port:container_port`.

levels.title: Trivy — Filesystem Scan
levels.content:
# Trivy — Filesystem Scan

In this level you will use Trivy to scan a project directory for vulnerable dependencies and hardcoded secrets.

## Scenario

You have cloned a web application repository. Before the code is merged to the main branch, you want to verify that no dependency contains a known CVE and that no developer accidentally committed an API key or password.

---

## Step 1 — Install Trivy

**Option A — Docker (no install needed):**
```bash
docker run --rm -v $(pwd):/project aquasec/trivy:latest filesystem /project
```

**Option B — Native install on Debian/Ubuntu:**
```bash
wget -qO - https://aquasecurity.github.io/trivy-repo/deb/public.key | apt-key add -
echo "deb https://aquasecurity.github.io/trivy-repo/deb generic main" | \
  tee /etc/apt/sources.list.d/trivy.list
apt-get update && apt-get install -y trivy
```

## Step 2 — Scan for Vulnerable Dependencies

```bash
trivy filesystem \
  --severity HIGH,CRITICAL \
  --format table \
  .
```

Trivy will detect languages automatically (Python pip, Node npm, Java Maven/Gradle, Go modules, etc.) and match each package against its CVE database.

## Step 3 — Scan for Hardcoded Secrets

```bash
trivy filesystem \
  --scanners secret \
  --format table \
  .
```

Trivy detects patterns such as AWS access keys, GitHub tokens, private keys, and generic high-entropy strings.

## Step 4 — Export a Machine-Readable Report

```bash
trivy filesystem \
  --scanners vuln,secret \
  --severity HIGH,CRITICAL \
  --format json \
  --output trivy-report.json \
  .
```

## Understanding the Output

Each finding in the report contains:

| Field | Description |
|---|---|
| `VulnerabilityID` | The CVE identifier (e.g. `CVE-2023-32681`) |
| `PkgName` | The affected library name |
| `InstalledVersion` | The version currently in use |
| `FixedVersion` | The version that patches the vulnerability |
| `Severity` | CRITICAL / HIGH / MEDIUM / LOW |

---

> **Answer:** What is the value passed to `--format` in Step 4 to produce a machine-readable report file?
levels.solution:
The command in Step 4 uses `--format json` to produce a machine-readable report.

Submit **`json`** as the answer.

hints.title: Look at the export command
hints.content: In Step 4, the `--format` flag is used to choose the output format. The value that produces a structured machine-readable file is `json`.

levels.title: Trivy — Container Image Scan
levels.content:
# Trivy — Container Image Scan

In this level you will scan a Docker container image to identify CVEs in OS packages and language libraries bundled inside the image.

## Scenario

Your team has built a Docker image for a web service. Before pushing it to the production registry, you need to scan the image to ensure it does not contain any HIGH or CRITICAL vulnerabilities that have a known fix available.

---

## Step 1 — Scan a Public Image (Basic)

```bash
trivy image nginx:latest
```

This scans the `nginx:latest` image and prints a table of all CVEs found in its OS packages.

## Step 2 — Filter by Severity

Focus only on actionable findings:

```bash
trivy image \
  --severity HIGH,CRITICAL \
  nginx:latest
```

## Step 3 — Skip Vulnerabilities With No Fix

Some CVEs have no patch available yet. Skip them to focus on what you can actually fix:

```bash
trivy image \
  --severity HIGH,CRITICAL \
  --ignore-unfixed \
  nginx:latest
```

## Step 4 — Fail the Pipeline if Vulnerabilities Found

In a CI/CD pipeline, use `--exit-code 1` to fail the job automatically:

```bash
trivy image \
  --severity HIGH,CRITICAL \
  --ignore-unfixed \
  --exit-code 1 \
  --format json \
  --output trivy-container-report.json \
  nginx:latest
```

If any HIGH or CRITICAL fixable vulnerability is found, Trivy exits with code `1` and the pipeline fails — blocking the image from being pushed to production.

## Step 5 — Scan a Private Registry Image

```bash
trivy image \
  --username $REGISTRY_USER \
  --password $REGISTRY_PASSWORD \
  registry.yourcompany.com/myapp:latest
```

## Tips for Reducing CVEs in Container Images

| Strategy | Effect |
|---|---|
| Use minimal base images (e.g. `alpine`, `distroless`) | Reduces OS attack surface drastically |
| Pin base image versions and update regularly | Ensures you get patched OS packages |
| Run as non-root user | Limits impact of exploitation |
| Remove build tools from final image | Use multi-stage Docker builds |

---

> **Answer:** What is the Trivy flag (from Step 3) that skips CVEs which have no patch available yet?
levels.solution:
The flag `--ignore-unfixed` tells Trivy to skip vulnerabilities for which no fixed version exists in the package repository.

Submit **`--ignore-unfixed`** as the answer.

hints.title: Look at Step 3
hints.content: Step 3 adds one extra flag to the command from Step 2. That flag filters out CVEs that cannot be fixed because no patched version is available yet.

levels.title: Reading Results and Generating Reports
levels.content:
# Reading Results and Generating Reports

## SonarQube — Reading the Dashboard

After a scan completes, the SonarQube project dashboard shows:

| Panel | What It Shows |
|---|---|
| **Quality Gate** | Overall PASS or FAIL for new code |
| **Reliability** | Bugs — rating A–E |
| **Security** | Vulnerabilities — rating A–E |
| **Maintainability** | Code smells and technical debt |
| **Coverage** | Percentage of lines covered by tests |
| **Duplications** | Percentage of duplicated code blocks |

Each issue includes: severity, rule ID, explanation, and a remediation example.

## SonarQube — Exporting Reports via API

```bash
# Get all CRITICAL/BLOCKER issues for a project
curl -u YOUR_TOKEN: \
  "http://localhost:9000/api/issues/search\
?projectKeys=my-project&severity=CRITICAL,BLOCKER"

# Get project metrics (bugs, vulns, coverage, debt)
curl -u YOUR_TOKEN: \
  "http://localhost:9000/api/measures/component\
?component=my-project\
&metricKeys=bugs,vulnerabilities,code_smells,coverage,security_rating"

# Get Quality Gate result
curl -u YOUR_TOKEN: \
  "http://localhost:9000/api/qualitygates/project_status\
?projectKey=my-project"
```

## Trivy — Output Formats

| Format | Command Flag | Best For |
|---|---|---|
| Table | `--format table` | Human review in terminal |
| JSON | `--format json` | Automation, custom scripts |
| SARIF | `--format sarif` | GitLab / GitHub Security Dashboard |
| HTML | `--format template --template @contrib/html.tpl` | Shareable report |
| CycloneDX | `--format cyclonedx` | Software Bill of Materials (SBOM) |

```bash
# HTML report from an image scan
trivy image \
  --format template \
  --template "@contrib/html.tpl" \
  --output trivy-report.html \
  nginx:latest

# SARIF for GitLab Security Dashboard
trivy filesystem \
  --format sarif \
  --output trivy-fs.sarif \
  .
```

## GitLab Security Dashboard Integration

Declare Trivy output as a GitLab artifact to automatically populate the Security Dashboard:

```yaml
trivy-scan:
  script:
    - trivy image --format sarif --output trivy.sarif myapp:latest
  artifacts:
    reports:
      container_scanning: trivy.sarif
```

Access findings at: **Project → Security & Compliance → Security Dashboard**

## Summary — What Each Tool Gives You

| Tool | What You Get |
|---|---|
| **SonarQube** | Code quality score, vulnerability list mapped to OWASP/CWE, quality gate pass/fail, historical trend |
| **Trivy** | CVE list per package with fixed versions, secret findings, SBOM, container image risk |

> Running both tools together gives you a **complete SAST picture**: your own code quality (SonarQube) and the security of everything you depend on (Trivy).
